--

“JavaScript doesn’t execute your code immediately. It first prepares everything it needs before running a single line.”

If you’ve ever wondered:

• Why can you call a function before it’s declared?

• Why does var return undefined instead of throwing an error?

• Why does let behave differently from var?

• How does recursion work?
The answer to all of these lies in one of JavaScript’s most fundamental concepts: Execution Context.
Whether you’re preparing for JavaScript interviews, building React applications, or simply trying to understand how JavaScript works internally, mastering execution contexts is essential.
Let’s explore this concept with real-world examples.
What is an Execution Context?
An Execution Context is the environment where JavaScript executes your code. Think of it as a workspace that JavaScript creates whenever it starts executing code. This workspace contains everything JavaScript needs to run your program:

• Variables

• Functions

• Function parameters

• Scope information

• The this keyword

• References to outer scopes
Every piece of JavaScript code runs inside an execution context.
A Real-World Analogy: A Restaurant Kitchen
Imagine you’re at a restaurant. When you place an order, the chef doesn’t immediately start cooking. Instead, the chef first:

• Reads the order.

• Collects all the ingredients.

• Prepares the utensils.

• Organizes the cooking station.

• Then starts cooking.
JavaScript works in exactly the same way. Before executing your code, it first prepares everything it needs. That preparation stage is called the Execution Context.
Types of Execution Context :
avaScript creates three different types of execution contexts.
1. Global Execution Context
This is the first execution context created when your JavaScript program starts. Example:
const appName = "Medium";
function greet() {
console.log("Welcome!");
}
greet();When the script loads, JavaScript creates the Global Execution Context. It stores:

• Global variables

• Global functions

• Global this
There is only one Global Execution Context for each JavaScript program.
2. Function Execution Context
Every time you call a function, JavaScript creates a brand new execution context for that function.
function add(a, b) {
return a + b;
}add(10, 20);
When add() is called, JavaScript creates a separate execution context containing:

• Parameters (a, b)

• Local variables

• Function scope

• Return value
Calling the function again creates another fresh execution context.
3. Eval Execution Context
Whenever eval() is used, JavaScript creates another execution context.
eval("console.log('Hello')");Although it exists, eval() is generally discouraged because it can introduce security and performance issues.
The Two Phases of an Execution Context
Every execution context goes through two phases:

• Memory Creation Phase

• Code Execution Phase
Understanding these two phases explains many JavaScript behaviors.
Phase 1: Memory Creation Phase
Before executing any code, JavaScript scans the entire file. During this phase, it allocates memory. Consider this example:
console.log(name);var name = "Krishna";
function greet() {
console.log("Hello");
}
Before executing anything, JavaScript creates memory like this:
name → undefinedgreet → Entire function
Notice that:

• Variables declared with var are initialized with undefined.

• Function declarations are stored completely in memory.
No code has been executed yet.
Phase 2: Code Execution Phase
Once memory allocation is complete, JavaScript starts executing the code line by line. Execution proceeds like this:
console.log(name); //output : undefined Then JavaScript executes:
name = "Krishna";Finally:
greet(); // output : HelloSince the function already existed in memory, JavaScript could execute it immediately.
Understanding Hoisting:
Execution context is the reason hoisting exists. Example
console.log(city);var city = "Bangalore";
Output:
undefinedThis happens because during memory creation: City → Undefined Later, during execution: City→"Banglore"
Now compare it with let.
console.log(city); // Output: ReferenceErrorlet city = "Bangalore";
Variables declared with let and const are also hoisted, but they remain inside the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) until their declaration is reached.
Function Hoisting :
Function declarations behave differently.
sayHello();function sayHello() {
console.log("Hello"); // Output: Hello
}
This works because JavaScript stores the entire function during the memory creation phase. However, function expressions behave differently.
sayHello(); // ReferenceErrorconst sayHello = function () {
console.log("Hello");
};
Since sayHello is declared with const, it cannot be accessed before initialization.
Execution Context and the Call Stack
Execution contexts are managed using the Call Stack. Let’s look at an example.
function one() {
two();
}function two() {
three();
}
function three() {
console.log("Done");
}
one();
Execution order:
Global → one() → two() → three()After three() finishes:
three() removed → two() removed → one() removed → Global remainsThe Call Stack follows the Last In, First Out (LIFO) principle.
Real-World Example: Stacking Plates 🍽️:
Imagine you’re stacking plates in your kitchen. You place them one on top of another:
Plate C
─────────
Plate B
─────────
Plate ANow, when it’s time to remove them, you can’t remove Plate A first. You must remove them in reverse order:
Remove Plate C ✅
↓Remove Plate B ✅
↓
Remove Plate A ✅
This is called LIFO (Last In, First Out). The JavaScript Call Stack works exactly the same way. Every function call is placed on top of the stack, and the most recently added function must finish before the previous one can continue.
Execution Context During Recursion:
Every recursive call creates a new execution context. Example
function countdown(number) {
if (number === 0) return; console.log(number);
countdown(number - 1);
}
countdown(3);
Call Stack:
Global↓
countdown(3)
↓
countdown(2)
↓
countdown(1)
After reaching the base case:
countdown(1)↓
countdown(2)
↓
countdown(3)
↓
Global
Every recursive function call gets its own execution context.
What Does an Execution Context Contain?
Each execution context stores:

• Variable Environment

• Lexical Environment

• Scope Chain

• Function Arguments

• The this keyword

• Reference to the outer environment
These components enable JavaScript features such as:

• Closures

• Lexical Scoping

• Hoisting

• this Binding

• Nested Functions
A Real-Time Example: Online Food Delivery
Imagine you order food using a delivery app.
Before the restaurant starts cooking, it performs several steps:

• Verifies the order.

• Checks ingredient availability.

• Assigns a chef.

• Allocates a delivery partner.

• Confirms payment.
Only after these preparations does cooking begin.
Similarly, JavaScript first creates an execution context, allocates memory, prepares variables and functions, and only then starts executing your code.
Performance Considerations
Each function call creates a new execution context.
Deep recursion or excessive nested function calls can:

• Increase memory consumption.

• Grow the call stack.

• Cause a RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded.
Understanding execution contexts helps you write cleaner, more efficient JavaScript.
Key Takeaways

• Every JavaScript program starts with a Global Execution Context.

• Every function call creates a new Function Execution Context.

• Each execution context has two phases:

• Memory Creation Phase

• Code Execution Phase

• Hoisting occurs because JavaScript allocates memory before executing code.

• Execution contexts are managed using the Call Stack, which follows the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle.

• Understanding execution contexts makes concepts like hoisting, closures, recursion, lexical scope, and this much easier to understand.
Conclusion
The JavaScript Execution Context is one of the most important concepts in the language because it explains what happens behind the scenes before your code runs. It is the foundation for understanding hoisting, the call stack, scope, closures, recursion, and asynchronous programming.
Instead of memorizing JavaScript behavior, understanding execution contexts allows you to reason about why the language behaves the way it does. Once you master this concept, many advanced JavaScript topics become significantly easier to learn.
Understanding how JavaScript thinks is often more valuable than simply knowing how to write JavaScript. That’s what separates a good developer from a great one.
